title: CDN, DNS & Networking
summary: How CDNs work, DNS resolution, TCP/IP fundamentals, anycast, and network-level optimizations.

sections.content:
## Content Delivery Networks (CDN)

A CDN is a geographically distributed network of servers (PoPs — Points of Presence) that cache content close to users. Instead of every request crossing oceans to reach your origin server, it's served from the nearest PoP.

**What CDNs serve:**
- Static assets (images, CSS, JS, fonts)
- Video/streaming content
- API responses (with proper cache headers)
- Full web pages (edge-side rendering)

sections.title: CDN Request Flow
sections.content:
graph LR
    U1[User in Tokyo] -->|1. Request| PoP1[CDN PoP Tokyo]
    U2[User in London] -->|1. Request| PoP2[CDN PoP London]
    PoP1 -->|2. Cache HIT| U1
    PoP2 -->|2. Cache MISS| Origin[Origin Server\nUS East]
    Origin -->|3. Fetch & cache| PoP2
    PoP2 -->|4. Serve| U2
    style PoP1 fill:#059669,color:#fff
    style Origin fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff

## DNS Resolution

DNS translates human-readable domain names to IP addresses. Resolution is hierarchical and cached.

```
Browser: What is the IP of api.example.com?

1. Check browser cache → miss
2. Check OS cache (hosts file) → miss  
3. Query Recursive Resolver (ISP or 8.8.8.8)
4. Recursive Resolver queries:
   a. Root nameserver → "Try .com nameservers"
   b. .com TLD nameserver → "Try example.com nameservers"
   c. example.com nameserver → "api.example.com = 1.2.3.4"
5. Return 1.2.3.4, cache with TTL
```

### DNS Record Types
| Record | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | Domain → IPv4 | api.example.com → 1.2.3.4 |
| **AAAA** | Domain → IPv6 | api.example.com → 2001:db8::1 |
| **CNAME** | Domain → Domain | www → example.com |
| **MX** | Mail server | → mail.example.com |
| **TXT** | Arbitrary text | SPF, DKIM verification |
| **NS** | Nameserver delegation | example.com → ns1.example.com |

sections.title: DNS Resolution Hierarchy
sections.content:
graph TD
    Browser -->|1. query| Resolver[Recursive Resolver\n8.8.8.8]
    Resolver -->|2. who handles .com?| Root[Root Nameserver]
    Root -->|3. try a.gtld-servers.net| Resolver
    Resolver -->|4. who handles example.com?| TLD[.com TLD Nameserver]
    TLD -->|5. try ns1.example.com| Resolver
    Resolver -->|6. what is api.example.com?| Auth[example.com\nAuthoritative NS]
    Auth -->|7. 1.2.3.4 TTL 300| Resolver
    Resolver -->|8. 1.2.3.4| Browser
    style Auth fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style Browser fill:#059669,color:#fff

## TCP Fundamentals

### Three-way Handshake
```
Client → SYN        → Server   (I want to connect)
Client ← SYN-ACK    ← Server   (OK, I'm ready)
Client → ACK        → Server   (Great, let's go)
         [data transfer]
Client → FIN        → Server   (I'm done)
Client ← FIN-ACK    ← Server   (Acknowledged)
```

### HTTP/1.1 vs HTTP/2 vs HTTP/3
| Version | Key Improvement |
|---|---|
| HTTP/1.1 | Persistent connections, keep-alive |
| HTTP/2 | Multiplexing (multiple requests on 1 TCP connection), header compression |
| HTTP/3 | Built on QUIC (UDP-based), eliminates TCP head-of-line blocking |

## Anycast

Anycast routes traffic to the **nearest node** that shares the same IP address — used by CDNs and DNS providers (Cloudflare's 1.1.1.1, Google's 8.8.8.8) for global distribution without client-side configuration.

sections.question: What is the main performance benefit of HTTP/2 over HTTP/1.1?
options: HTTP/2 uses UDP instead of TCP
HTTP/2 supports multiplexing — multiple requests/responses over a single TCP connection simultaneously
HTTP/2 compresses response bodies more efficiently
sections.explanation: HTTP/1.1 requires separate TCP connections for parallel requests (or serial requests per connection). HTTP/2 multiplexing allows multiple request/response streams over a single connection simultaneously, eliminating connection overhead and head-of-line blocking at the HTTP layer.
